content.text: Per-tenant prompt caching namespacing means every cached Claude prefix, cached response, or cached semantic match must be partitioned by tenant so one tenant can never benefit from, or leak into, another tenant’s cache entry. In a Claude backend, that is especially important because prompt caching is often driven by repeated system prompts, tool schemas, and static context, which are exactly the kinds of things that can accidentally become cross-tenant if you key them too broadly.

Prompt caching is only safe when the cache boundary matches the authorization boundary. If your app serves multiple tenants with different policies, tools, personas, or retrieval sources, then a cache key that ignores tenant ID can return the wrong prompt prefix, the wrong tool configuration, or even the wrong answer artifacts.

The expert rule is simple: if tenant A should not see tenant B’s conversation history, tenant A should not share tenant B’s cache namespace either. That applies to exact caches, semantic caches, provider prefix caches, and any hybrid layering you build on top.
You should namespace all of the following by tenant, and often by user, session, prompt version, and model version:
Often reused across requests and tenants
Different tenants may expose different tools
Can leak tenant-private data if shared
Replaying answers directly is highest risk

The safest implementation is to treat tenant ID as a required dimension of the cache key, not an optional tag. Several caching guides explicitly warn that the boundary must include tenant ID and permissions, because cross-tenant cache leakage is one of the most dangerous failure modes.
Why Claude prompt caching is tricky

Claude prompt caching rewards stable prefixes: the system prompt, tool definitions, and other repeated instructions can be cached efficiently when they appear at the beginning of the prompt. That is great for cost and latency, but it creates a design tension in multi-tenant systems because the very best cacheable parts are often tenant-specific: org rules, tools, safety policy, or RAG context.

If you put tenant ID inside the stable prefix, you get separate provider-side caches per tenant naturally. If you put tenant ID only in the dynamic tail, the provider may reuse the prefix across tenants when it should not, depending on your exact caching strategy and how much of the prompt is considered stable.

The practical design lesson is:
- Keep tenant-specific instructions in the cacheable prefix only if they must be isolated per tenant.
- Keep highly dynamic per-user or per-session content out of the cacheable prefix when possible.
A robust cache key usually looks like:
For semantic caches, add permission-sensitive boundaries too:

This mirrors production guidance that exact caching should hash the normalized request plus parameters, while semantic caching must always preserve permission boundaries and tenant scope. Future-facing caching writeups also recommend versioning prompt, embedding, and index versions so changes invalidate the right entries automatically.

This is the safest kind. You hash the full normalized request and all model parameters, then return the stored response only if everything matches exactly. In multi-tenant systems, exact cache keys still need tenant scope; otherwise identical prompts from two tenants become indistinguishable.

This is the pattern Claude prompt caching is most associated with: keep stable instructions first, mark them cacheable, and reuse that prefix across repeated calls. In a multi-tenant backend, you can make the prefix tenant-specific by including the tenant identity, org policies, and tool schemas in the stable prefix so each tenant gets its own cache line.

This matches by meaning rather than byte-for-byte equality. That makes it powerful, but also riskier, because “similar enough” can cross permission boundaries unless the namespace includes tenant and scope data and the gateway enforces the boundary before any vector lookup happens.

For regulated or enterprise systems, the safest stance is: exact caching for high-risk flows, prefix caching for stable tenant-specific system instructions, and semantic caching only with strict gateway enforcement and scoped namespaces.
A clean architecture usually has these layers:
verifies JWT and resolves trusted tenant context.
determines which prompt version and tool set a tenant can use.
composes namespace using tenant, user, feature, prompt version, and model version.
checks exact or prefix cache entries inside that namespace.
runs the model only on cache miss.
records hits, misses, and any cross-tenant rejection attempts.
This keeps cache safety close to authorization, which is where it belongs.
There are two different kinds of caching, and they need different controls.
: Claude reuses computation for repeated prompt prefixes, usually system instructions and other stable content.
: your backend stores full answers or partial artifacts and returns them later without calling Claude at all.

Provider-side caching is mainly a performance and cost optimization, but app-side caching is also a security boundary. App-side cache keys must be stricter because a wrong hit can directly leak another tenant’s response.
Tenant ID is mandatory in every cache key constructor.
Prompt versions invalidate old entries automatically.
Tool schemas are cached per tenant and per environment.
Semantic caches are filtered by permission scope before matching.
Isolation tests intentionally replay the same prompt under two tenant contexts and assert different cache entries.
One global prefix cache for all tenants.
Same cache key for support chat and finance chat.
Reusing a cached system prompt that contains one tenant’s org rules in another tenant’s request.
Relying on TTL alone instead of namespace boundaries.
is required for response replay caches.
separates chat, summarization, extraction, and tools.
Semantic caching is disabled for high-risk prompts unless explicitly approved.
That policy is strict enough to prevent leakage while still allowing cost savings where they are safe.

The most valuable test is an isolation test:
1. Run the same prompt under Tenant A.
2. Run the same prompt under Tenant B.
3. Verify the cache entries are different.
4. Verify Tenant B cannot receive Tenant A’s cached prefix, semantic match, or replayed response.

You should also test:
- Prompt version changes invalidate prior caches.
- Tool schema changes invalidate prior caches.
- Permissions changes invalidate prior semantic matches.
- A user in Tenant A cannot hit a cache entry generated by Tenant B, even if the text is identical.

Think of caching like a kitchen prep station. You want to reuse chopped onions for the same restaurant order, but you would never put the same prep bowl back on the line for a different restaurant’s order if the recipes, dietary rules, or ingredients differ. Per-tenant namespacing is the label on the bowl that stops the kitchen from mixing restaurants.
Tenant ID is part of every cache namespace and constructor.
Prompt version, model version, and tool version are explicit cache dimensions.
Stable tenant-specific instructions live in the cacheable prefix.
Dynamic user/session data stays outside the shared prefix whenever possible.
Semantic caches are filtered by tenant and permission before matching.
High-risk prompts either bypass caches or use exact-match only.
Isolation tests are part of CI and release gating.
Why does tenant ID belong in the cache key?
Because cache boundaries must match authorization boundaries, or one tenant can receive another tenant’s cached data.
What is the safest caching method?
Exact-match caching with strict tenant and permission scoping.
What makes prefix caching useful in Claude?
Claude can reuse repeated stable prefixes like system instructions and tool schemas, which lowers latency and cost.
Because approximate matching can cross user or tenant boundaries unless namespacing and permission filtering are enforced first.
A global cache for all tenants with no versioning and no permission scope.
